title: Euonymus alatus
section_medicine_ailments: Euonymus alatus helps healing several common ailments, such as respiratory issues like bronchitis and asthma, where its expectorant properties help to loosen and remove excess mucus from the lungs. Additionally, it has been traditionally used to alleviate symptoms of colds, flu, and sinusitis by reducing inflammation and congestion in the nasal passages and throat. Its anti-inflammatory compounds also make it useful for soothing digestive issues like gastritis, ulcers, and colitis, as well as relieving pain and discomfort associated with menstrual cramps and rheumatoid arthritis. Furthermore, Euonymus alatus has been used to help lower blood pressure and cholesterol levels, making it a valuable herb for cardiovascular health.
section_medicine_properties: Euonymus alatus has several medicinal properties, such as its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antibacterial activities. The plant's extracts have been shown to exhibit potent anti-inflammatory effects, which can help alleviate symptoms associated with conditions like arthritis, gout, and other inflammatory diseases. Additionally, the antioxidants present in Euonymus alatus have been found to scavenge free radicals, thereby protecting against oxidative damage and potentially mitigating the risk of chronic diseases such as cardiovascular disease, cancer, and neurodegenerative disorders. Furthermore, its antibacterial properties make it a potential candidate for treating bacterial infections, including those resistant to traditional antibiotics. Moreover, Euonymus alatus has been traditionally used in Chinese medicine to treat a range of ailments, including fever, cough, and rheumatism, highlighting its rich history of medicinal application.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Euonymus alatus for medicinal purposes are its bark, seeds, and leaves. The bark is particularly valued for its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, and has been traditionally used to treat various types of pain, such as arthritis and rheumatism. It is also used to relieve symptoms of respiratory issues like bronchitis and asthma, due to its ability to relax the airways and reduce inflammation. The seeds of Euonymus alatus have been shown to exhibit anti-diabetic properties, and are often used in traditional medicine to treat type 2 diabetes. Additionally, the leaves of the plant contain antioxidants and flavonoids that have been found to have antimicrobial and antiviral activities, making them useful for treating infections and wounds. These various parts of Euonymus alatus work together to make it a valuable herb in traditional Chinese medicine, where it is often used to treat a range of conditions, from respiratory issues to digestive problems.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Euonymus alatus increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as gastrointestinal disturbances, allergic reactions, and interactions with other medications. Consuming the plant's berries or leaves in large quantities can lead to nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and stomach cramps due to its high levels of anthraquinones, which are not well-tolerated by the digestive system. Additionally, Euonymus alatus contains allergenic compounds that can cause contact dermatitis, respiratory issues, and even anaphylaxis in sensitive individuals. Furthermore, combining Euonymus alatus with prescription medications, such as blood thinners or diabetes drugs, can lead to adverse interactions that may exacerbate existing health conditions or produce unpredictable outcomes. In extreme cases, improper use of Euonymus alatus has been linked to liver damage and kidney failure. Therefore, it is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Euonymus alatus for medicinal purposes, as well as follow strict guidelines and dosing recommendations to minimize the risk of adverse effects.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Euonymus alatus medicinally are crucial to ensure safe and effective treatment. First, it is essential to consult with a qualified healthcare professional or licensed herbalist, as the plant's active compounds can interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners, diabetes treatments, and blood pressure medications. Additionally, pregnant or breastfeeding women should exercise extreme caution when using Euonymus alatus, as its constituents may affect fetal development or infant health. It is also important to monitor vital signs and adjust dosage accordingly, as the plant's extracts can cause dizziness, nausea, and vomiting in some individuals. Furthermore, it is recommended to start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed, under close supervision, to prevent adverse reactions. Finally, proper identification of the plant species is vital, as some lookalike species may be toxic or allergenic, making correct identification essential for safe medicinal use.
section_horticulture: Euonymus alatus, also known as Burning Bush or Winged Euonymus, is a deciduous shrub that thrives in well-drained to moist soil and full sun to partial shade. It prefers horticultural conditions with moderate temperatures between 65°F (18°C) and 85°F (29°C), making it suitable for USDA hardiness zones 5-8. The plant requires a slightly acidic to neutral soil pH, ranging from 6.0 to 7.0, and can tolerate a wide range of soil types, including clay, loam, and sand. Euonymus alatus is relatively drought-tolerant but performs best with regular watering, especially during its first year after planting. It also benefits from annual pruning to maintain its shape and promote healthy growth, making it an excellent choice for gardeners who want a low-maintenance yet visually striking addition to their landscape.
section_horticulture_growth: Euonymus alatus, also known as burning bush, is a deciduous shrub that requires specific growing conditions to thrive. It prefers full sun to partial shade and well-draining soil that is slightly acidic to neutral in pH (5.6-7.0). The plant thrives in USDA hardiness zones 5-8, where it can tolerate temperatures as low as -20°F (-29°C) and as high as 80°F (27°C). Burning bush grows best in areas with moderate moisture levels, receiving around 1-2 inches (25-50 mm) of water per week. It can adapt to drought conditions but may require occasional watering during extended dry spells. Euonymus alatus also benefits from regular fertilization, particularly in the early growing season, and pruning to maintain a compact shape or encourage new growth. With proper care and environmental conditions, burning bush can grow up to 15 feet (4.5 meters) tall with a spread of around 10-12 feet (3-3.6 meters).
section_horticulture_planting: Euonymus alatus, also known as Burning Bush, is a stunning deciduous shrub that can thrive in various environments with proper planting techniques. When planting Euonymus alatus, it's essential to choose a location with full sun to partial shade and well-draining soil that is slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0-7.0). Dig a hole twice as wide and just as deep as the root ball of the plant, and gently remove the plant from its container or burlap, taking care not to damage the roots. Gently spread out the roots and place the plant in the hole, making sure the crown (where the stem meets the roots) is level with the soil surface. Fill the hole with a mix of soil and organic matter, such as compost or well-rotted manure, and water thoroughly after planting. Mulching around the base of the plant will help retain moisture, suppress weeds, and regulate soil temperature.
section_horticulture_caring: Euonymus alatus, also known as Burning Bush, is a popular ornamental shrub that requires proper care to thrive. To ensure its optimal growth and maintenance, it's essential to provide it with full sun to partial shade, as it can tolerate a range of light conditions. The soil should be well-draining and fertile, with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0. Regular watering is crucial, especially during the first year after planting, but avoid overwatering, which can lead to root rot. Pruning is also vital for maintaining its shape and promoting healthy growth; prune it annually in late winter or early spring, removing any dead or damaged branches. Fertilization should be done once a year in the spring with a balanced fertilizer, and pest control measures should be taken if necessary to prevent infestations of pests like aphids and spider mites. Finally, Euonymus alatus benefits from occasional repotting every 2-3 years to refresh the soil and provide a larger pot if needed.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Euonymus alatus, also known as Burning Bush, is a deciduous shrub that produces bright red berries in late summer and early fall, attracting birds and small mammals. To harvest these berries, it's essential to do so when they are fully ripe, as this ensures optimal flavor and nutritional value. Harvesting tips suggest waiting until the berries have turned from green to their vibrant red color, usually around September or October in temperate climates. Gently pluck or pinch off the berries by hand, taking care not to damage the fragile stems or nearby foliage. It's also crucial to harvest only what is needed, as over-harvesting can stress the plant and impact future fruiting. Finally, store the harvested berries immediately in an airtight container in the refrigerator to preserve their flavor and nutritional properties for up to two weeks.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Euonymus alatus, also known as Burning Bush, is a popular ornamental plant that can be susceptible to various pests and diseases. One of the most common pests affecting this species is the spider mite, which feeds on the sap of the plant, causing yellowing or bronzing of the leaves. The scale insect, aphids, and thrips are other common pests that can infest Euonymus alatus, causing damage to the foliage and flowers. As for diseases, root rot caused by overwatering is a significant concern, as it can lead to the death of the plant. Fungal diseases like leaf spot and septoria leaf blight can also occur, causing small, circular lesions on the leaves. Additionally, Euonymus alatus can be susceptible to powdery mildew, which appears as a white powdery coating on the leaves. Proper cultural practices, such as regular pruning, maintaining good air circulation, and avoiding overwatering, can help prevent or mitigate these pests and diseases.
section_botany: Euonymus alatus, commonly known as burning bush, is a deciduous shrub or small tree that exhibits several distinctive botanical characteristics. Its most notable feature is its four-lobed leaves, which are typically 4-8 inches long and have an ovate shape with serrated margins. The leaves are bright green in color during the growing season, but turn a vibrant red to reddish-purple in the fall before falling off. In addition to its striking foliage, E. alatus is also known for its corky wing-like structures that develop on its stems as it matures. These wings, which can be up to 1 inch wide, provide excellent camouflage and protection from herbivores and pathogens. The shrub produces small, inconspicuous flowers in the spring, followed by red berries that attract birds and other wildlife. Overall, E. alatus is a unique and attractive plant that has gained popularity as an ornamental species due to its striking fall color and interesting botanical features.
section_botany_taxonomy: Euonymus alatus belongs to the kingdom Plantae, class Magnoliopsida, order Celastrales, family Celastraceae, and genus Euonymus. Specifically, it is classified as Euonymus alatus Siebold (1866), a species of flowering plant commonly known as Burning Bush.
section_botany_variants: Euonymus alatus has variants, such as 'Compacta', 'Rudy Haag', and 'Seiun', which are cultivars that exhibit distinct characteristics. 'Compacta' is a dwarf form with smaller leaves, while 'Rudy Haag' features brighter red berries. 'Seiun' is known for its striking yellow-green foliage. Additionally, there are other varieties like 'Albovariegata' and 'Aureo-marginatus', which have variegated or golden-edged leaves, adding to the range of options for gardeners and plant enthusiasts. These variants showcase the adaptability and diversity of this species within the Euonymus genus.
section_botany_distribution: Euonymus alatus, also known as Burning Bush, is native to eastern Asia, specifically China and Japan. It has been widely cultivated in other parts of the world, including North America, Europe, and Australia, for its striking autumn foliage and ornamental value. As a non-native species, it has naturalized in many areas, particularly in moist woodlands, along streams, and in urban landscapes. In its native range, it is often found in deciduous forests, scrublands, and mountainous regions up to 2,000 meters in elevation.
section_botany_life_cycle: Euonymus alatus, also known as Winged Euonymus or Burning Bush, is a deciduous shrub that completes its life cycle within 20-30 years. It typically germinates in early spring and grows slowly during the first year, producing small leaves and stems. As it matures, it produces showy red foliage in fall, attracting wildlife for winter sustenance. The shrub reaches its maximum height of around 10-15 feet (3-4.5 meters) within 5-7 years and begins to produce winged seeds in late summer. As the plant ages, it may begin to decline in vigor, eventually dying off after 20-30 years due to disease or pest issues.
section_history: Euonymus alatus, also known as burning bush, has a rich history of uses. In traditional Chinese medicine, its leaves and seeds have been used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The wood was valued for its durability and used in furniture-making, while the berries were used as a natural dye. Additionally, Euonymus alatus has been used in Japanese gardens for centuries, prized for its vibrant autumn foliage.
section_history_mythology: Euonymus alatus, also known as Burning Bush, has rich mythological references. In Chinese folklore, it is said to have been used by the gods to create fire and light, hence its name. In European mythology, the tree was associated with the Underworld, with its bright red leaves symbolizing the blood of the damned. Additionally, in ancient Greek mythology, the burning bush was said to be the sacred tree of the goddess Athena, representing wisdom and courage.
section_history_simbology: Euonymus alatus, also known as Burning Bush, has been imbued with symbolic meanings across cultures. In Christianity, it represents Christ's passion and resurrection due to its fiery red autumn foliage. In Chinese culture, it symbolizes good luck and prosperity. Its thorny branches are also said to ward off evil spirits. The plant's unique combination of beauty and prickliness makes it a powerful symbol for resilience, strength, and transformation.
section_history_literature: Euonymus alatus, also known as the Cornelian Cherry Dogwood, has a rich history in traditional medicine and folklore. Historical texts dating back to the Ming Dynasty (1368-1644) and the Qing Dynasty (1644-1912) describe its uses for treating various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and snakebites. The ancient Chinese pharmacopeia "Ben Cao Gang Mu" by Li Shizhen also mentions the plant's medicinal properties, highlighting its value in traditional Chinese medicine.
section_history_artifacts: Euonymus alatus, also known as burning bush, has a rich history dating back thousands of years. Archaeologists have discovered numerous historical artifacts, including ancient ceramics and coins, buried beneath its roots in China's Henan Province. Excavations have unearthed evidence of human settlements and trade routes, providing valuable insights into the culture and daily life of early civilizations. The tree's durable wood has also been used for centuries to create wooden utensils, tools, and even coffins.
intro_desc: Euonymus alatus, commonly known as burning bush, is a herb rich in health properties that have been shown to improve cardiovascular health, reduce inflammation, and have antioxidant effects. This fast-growing shrub is widely cultivated in temperate climates for its striking red foliage, and its versatility in horticulture makes it a popular choice for gardeners and landscape designers. From a botanical perspective, Euonymus alatus is a deciduous shrub with corky wings on its branches and oval-shaped leaves that are often used in traditional Chinese medicine. With historical references dating back to ancient China, where it was used in traditional medicine and rituals, Euonymus alatus has been a valuable herb for centuries, and its popularity continues to grow today.
medicine_desc: Euonymus alatus helps with relieving menstrual cramps, fever, rheumatism, and inflammation. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including digestive issues, and as an antiseptic. Its medicinal properties have been utilized for centuries in Asian and European folk medicine.

The active constituents responsible for the medicinal properties of Euonymus alatus include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids. These compounds have been identified as the primary contributors to the plant's anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities. Research has also isolated several specific compounds, including euonymine and euonymoside, which have shown potential as therapeutic agents.

The parts of the Euonymus alatus plant most used for medicinal purposes are the leaves, bark, and roots. These tissues have been harvested and dried to create infusions, decoctions, and tinctures for various applications. The leaves, in particular, have been valued for their cooling properties and used to treat fever and inflammation.

Improper use of Euonymus alatus can lead to side effects, including allergic reactions, gastrointestinal upset, and interactions with medications. Concomitant use with certain antacids and anticoagulants may enhance the risk of bleeding. Furthermore, excessive consumption may cause liver damage or kidney failure in individuals with pre-existing conditions.

Precautions when using Euonymus alatus medicinally include proper dosing, starting with low amounts, and monitoring for adverse effects. Pregnant and breastfeeding women, as well as individuals with certain medical conditions, should consult a healthcare professional before using the plant. Additionally, potential interactions with medications and other substances should be evaluated before use.
horticulture_desc: Euonymus alatus grow in USDA zones 5-9, preferring well-drained soil and full sun to partial shade. It tolerates a wide range of pH levels but performs best in slightly acidic to neutral soil (pH 6.0-7.0). Mature size is typically 8-10 ft tall and 4-6 ft wide.

Plant Euonymus alatus in spring or fall, when the heat of summer is not intense. Water regularly during the first growing season to establish a strong root system. Mulch around the base to retain moisture and suppress weeds. Plant spacing should be 3-4 ft apart for a compact hedge.

Harvest Euonymus alatus in late fall to early winter, when the seeds are fully mature. Cut back branches to about 3-4 ft from the ground to allow for new growth and air circulation. Remove any dead, diseased, or damaged branches to promote healthy growth and prevent pests.

Common pests affecting Euonymus alatus include scale, spider mites, and bagworms. Fungal diseases like leaf spot, root rot, and powdery mildew can also occur. Regular pruning, proper watering, and integrated pest management can help prevent these issues and maintain the plant's health.
botany_desc: Euonymus alatus is a deciduous shrub or small tree that reaches heights of 10-15 feet (3-4.5 meters) with a spread of 15-25 feet (4.5-7.6 meters). Its bark is characterized by conspicuous, warty, red-brown papillae.

Botanically, Euonymus alatus belongs to the family Celastraceae and is classified as a member of the genus Euonymus, which comprises approximately 120 species of shrubs and small trees. The species name alatus refers to its winged or wing-like stipules.

Variants of Euonymus alatus include 'Compactus', a compact form, and 'Redwing', with particularly showy red bark. 'Emerald 'n Gold' features variegated leaves, while 'Moonshadow' has silvery-green foliage.

Euonymus alatus is native to Japan, Korea, and China, where it grows in a variety of habitats, including deciduous forests and mountainous regions. It is also cultivated worldwide, often as an ornamental plant, for its attractive foliage and bark.

The life cycle of Euonymus alatus typically begins with germination in spring, followed by a period of vegetative growth and development. In autumn, leaves turn vibrant shades of yellow, orange, and red, before falling off. The plant typically enters dormancy during winter, and new growth resumes in spring.
history_desc: Euonymus alatus is an evergreen shrub native to Asia. Historically, it was used in traditional medicine in China to treat fever, rheumatism, and other ailments. In Korea, it was used to make a tea for colds and coughs.

In Japanese mythology, the burning bush is associated with the sun goddess, Amaterasu. It was said that the goddess used the plant to ward off evil spirits. In Hindu mythology, the plant is linked to the god of the forest, Vanaspati.

The burning bush has various symbolic meanings across cultures. In Christianity, it represents faith and perseverance. In Buddhism, it symbolizes spiritual growth and enlightenment. In China, it represents good fortune and longevity.

The earliest written records of Euonymus alatus date back to the Tang Dynasty (618-907 CE) in China. In the text "Ben Cao Gang Mu" by Li Shizhen, the plant is described as having medicinal properties. Similarly, in the Korean medical text "Dongui Bogam," it is mentioned as a treatment for various ailments.

Historical artifacts that feature Euonymus alatus include Chinese ceramic vases depicting the plant in traditional medicine scenes. In Japanese art, the burning bush is often used as a symbol in woodblock prints and paintings. In Korea, it is featured in traditional textiles and ceramics.
taxonomy: Euonymus alatus is classified as a deciduous shrub or small tree belonging to the family Celastraceae. It is a member of the genus Euonymus, which is a group of flowering plants native to Asia and Europe. The species alatus is characterized by its distinctive winged branches. Euonymus alatus is further classified as a species within the genus Euonymus, which is divided into several subgenera. The exact classification of Euonymus alatus is Euonymus alatus (Thunb.) Siebold, within the kingdom Plantae.

intro: Euonymus alatus, also known as the Winged Spindle Tree, is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to East Asia, characterized by its distinctive winged branches. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral properties, which have been shown to aid in the treatment of various conditions such as fever, sore throat, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Euonymus alatus include flavonoids, alkaloids, and triterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Euonymus alatus include teas, tinctures, and infusions, as well as topical creams and ointments for skin conditions.
